content.text: The term 'drainage' describes the river system of an area. It refers to the network of streams and rivers that flow in a region and drain water into larger water bodies such as lakes, seas, or oceans.

When observing a physical map, small streams flowing from different directions come together to form the main river, which ultimately drains into a large water body. This interconnected system of flowing water is what we call the drainage system of a region.
The area drained by a single river system is called a drainage basin. It encompasses all the land area whose water flows into a particular river.

An elevated area, such as a mountain or an upland, that separates two drainage basins. Water flowing on one side of a water divide belongs to one drainage basin, while water on the other side belongs to a different drainage basin.

A closer observation of maps indicates that any elevated area acts as a water divide, separating drainage basins. The overall shape and organization of drainage systems visible on maps are called drainage patterns.

The Ganga river system displays a dendritic drainage pattern, where tributaries resemble the branches of a tree spreading in different directions before meeting the main river. This pattern forms where terrain slopes uniformly in one direction.

The drainage systems of India are mainly controlled by the broad relief features of the subcontinent. Accordingly, Indian rivers are divided into two major groups: the Himalayan rivers and the Peninsular rivers.
Apart from originating from different physiographic regions, these two groups differ significantly in their characteristics, flow patterns, and influence on the landscape and human life.
Most Himalayan rivers are perennial, meaning they have water throughout the year. These rivers receive water from both rainfall and melted snow from the lofty mountains.

The two major Himalayan rivers, the Indus and the Brahmaputra, originate from the north of the mountain ranges. They have cut through the mountains, creating impressive gorges — deep, narrow valleys with steep sides.

Himalayan rivers have long courses from their source to the sea. In their upper courses, they perform intensive erosional activity, carrying huge loads of silt and sand. In the middle and lower courses, these rivers form meanders (snake-like bends), oxbow lakes (curved lakes formed when meanders are cut off), and many other depositional features in their floodplains. They also develop well-developed deltas at their mouths.

content.title: Himalayan River Features
content.text: The progression from upper to lower course shows a characteristic pattern: intense erosion in mountains → sediment transport in middle reaches → deposition and delta formation in lower reaches. This demonstrates how rivers reshape the landscape through these distinct zones.
A large number of Peninsular rivers are seasonal, as their flow is dependent on rainfall. During the dry season, even large rivers have reduced flow in their channels.

Peninsular rivers have shorter and shallower courses compared to their Himalayan counterparts. However, some originate in the central highlands and flow towards the west. Most rivers of peninsular India originate in the Western Ghats and flow towards the Bay of Bengal.

content.title: Contrast with Himalayan Rivers
content.text: While Himalayan rivers are perennial with long courses and substantial erosional power, Peninsular rivers are largely seasonal with shorter courses and more modest erosional capacity. The Peninsular plateau's stability and lack of high mountains result in different drainage characteristics.
The three major Himalayan rivers are the Indus, the Ganga, and the Brahmaputra. These rivers are long and are joined by many large and important tributaries, forming extensive river systems.
A river along with its tributaries is called a river system. The main river and all streams and rivers that join it form a unified drainage network.

The river Indus rises in Tibet, near Lake Mansarowar. Flowing west, it enters India in Ladakh, where it forms a picturesque gorge. With a total length of 2,900 km, the Indus is one of the longest rivers in the world.

Several tributaries join the Indus in the Kashmir region: the Zaskar, the Nubra, the Shyok, and the Hunza. Later, the Satluj, the Beas, the Ravi, the Chenab, and the Jhelum join together to enter the Indus near Mithankot in Pakistan.

Beyond Mithankot, the Indus flows southwards and eventually reaches the Arabian Sea east of Karachi. The Indus plain has a very gentle slope. A little over a third of the Indus basin is located in India (in Ladakh, Jammu and Kashmir, Himachal Pradesh, and Punjab), while the rest is in Pakistan.

content.title: Indus Waters Treaty (1960)
content.text: According to international regulations, India can use only 20 percent of the total water carried by the Indus river system. This water is used for irrigation in Punjab, Haryana, and the southern and western parts of Rajasthan.

The headwaters of the Ganga, called the 'Bhagirathi,' are fed by the Gangotri Glacier. The Bhagirathi is joined by the Alaknanda at Devaprayag in Uttarakhand. At Haridwar, the Ganga emerges from the mountains onto the plains.

The Ganga is joined by many important tributaries from the Himalayas. The Yamuna rises from the Yamunotri Glacier and flows parallel to the Ganga, meeting it as a right-bank tributary at Allahabad. The Ghaghara, the Gandak, and the Kosi rise in the Nepal Himalaya and are important tributaries that flood parts of the northern plains yearly, causing widespread damage but enriching soil for agricultural use.

Tributaries coming from peninsular uplands include the Chambal, the Betwa, and the Son. These rise from semi-arid areas, have shorter courses, and do not carry much water compared to Himalayan tributaries.

The Ganga flows eastwards till Farakka in West Bengal, the northernmost point of the Ganga delta. Here it bifurcates; the Bhagirathi-Hooghly (a distributary) flows southwards through the deltaic plains to the Bay of Bengal. The mainstream flows southwards into Bangladesh and is joined by the Brahmaputra. Further downstream, it is known as the Meghna, which flows into the Bay of Bengal.

The delta formed by the Ganga and Brahmaputra is known as the Sundarban Delta, the world's largest and fastest-growing delta. It is home to the Royal Bengal tiger. The length of the Ganga is over 2,500 km.

content.title: Ganga River Facts
content.text: The Ganga plain from Ambala to the Sundarban stretches over nearly 1,800 km, but the fall in elevation is hardly 300 metres — a fall of just one metre for every 6 km. This gentle slope causes the river to develop large meanders and is crucial for understanding the river's behavior and the formation of deltaic features.

content.title: Namami Gange Programme
content.text: An Integrated Conservation Mission approved as a flagship programme by the Union Government in June 2014 to accomplish effective pollution abatement, conservation, and rejuvenation of the Ganga river.

The Brahmaputra rises in Tibet, east of Mansarowar lake, very close to the sources of the Indus and Satluj. It is slightly longer than the Indus, though most of its course lies outside India. It flows eastwards parallel to the Himalayas.

On reaching the Namcha Barwa (7,757 m), the Brahmaputra takes a 'U' turn and enters India in Arunachal Pradesh through a gorge. Here, it is called the Dihang. It is joined by several tributaries including the Dibang, the Lohit, and many others to form the Brahmaputra in Assam.

content.title: Brahmaputra Names
content.text: The Brahmaputra is known as the Tsang Po in Tibet and Jamuna in Bangladesh, showing how river names change as they flow through different countries and regions.

In Tibet, the river carries a smaller volume of water and less silt as it flows through a cold, dry area. However, in India, it passes through a region of high rainfall. Here, the river carries a large volume of water and considerable amounts of silt.

The Brahmaputra has a braided channel throughout its length in Assam (a channel divided into multiple threads by islands) and forms many riverine islands. The world's largest riverine island, Majuli, is formed by the Brahmaputra.

Every year during the rainy season, the river overflows its banks, causing widespread devastation due to floods in Assam and Bangladesh. Unlike other north Indian rivers, the Brahmaputra is marked by huge deposits of silt on its bed, causing the riverbed to rise. The river also shifts its channel frequently, making it unpredictable.
The main water divide in Peninsular India is formed by the Western Ghats, which run from north to south close to the western coast. Most major rivers of the Peninsula originate in these highlands.
Most major rivers of the Peninsula — the Mahanadi, the Godavari, the Krishna, and the Kaveri — flow eastwards and drain into the Bay of Bengal. These rivers form deltas at their mouths.

There are numerous small streams flowing west of the Western Ghats. However, only two long rivers flow westwards: the Narmada and the Tapi. These rivers make estuaries (wider mouth areas where river meets sea) rather than deltas.
The drainage basins of peninsular rivers are comparatively smaller in size compared to Himalayan river basins.

The Narmada rises in the Amarkantak hills in Madhya Pradesh. It flows towards the west in a rift valley (a depression formed by geological faulting). On its way to the sea, the Narmada creates many picturesque locations.

The 'Marble rocks' near Jabalpur, where the Narmada flows through a deep gorge, and the 'Dhuadhar falls,' where the river plunges over steep rocks, are notable features. These demonstrate the river's erosional power despite being a peninsular river.
All tributaries of the Narmada are very short, and most join the main stream at right angles. The Narmada basin covers parts of Madhya Pradesh and Gujarat.

content.title: Namami Devi Narmade
content.text: A conservation mission undertaken by the government of Madhya Pradesh focusing on river conservation and environmental protection.

The Tapi rises in the Satpura ranges in the Betul district of Madhya Pradesh. It also flows in a rift valley parallel to the Narmada but is much shorter in length. Its basin covers parts of Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, and Maharashtra.

The coastal plains between the Western Ghats and the Arabian Sea are very narrow. Consequently, the coastal rivers are short. The main west-flowing rivers in this region are the Sabarmati, the Mahi, the Bharathpuzha, and the Periyar.

The Godavari is the largest Peninsular river. It rises from the slopes of the Western Ghats in the Nasik district of Maharashtra. Its length is about 1,500 km, making it the second-longest river in India after the Ganga.

The Godavari drains into the Bay of Bengal. Its drainage basin is the largest among peninsular rivers, covering parts of Maharashtra (about 50% of basin area), Madhya Pradesh, Odisha, and Andhra Pradesh.
The Godavari is joined by numerous tributaries including the Purna, the Wardha, the Pranhita, the Manjra, the Wainganga, and the Penganga. The last three are very large tributaries.

content.title: Dakshin Ganga
content.text: Due to its length and the area it covers, the Godavari is known as the 'Dakshin Ganga' (Ganga of the South), reflecting its importance to southern India.

The Mahanadi rises in the highlands of Chhattisgarh. It flows through Odisha to reach the Bay of Bengal. The length of the river is about 860 km. Its drainage basin is shared by Maharashtra, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, and Odisha.

The Krishna rises from a spring near Mahabaleshwar. It flows for about 1,400 km and reaches the Bay of Bengal. Important tributaries include the Tungabhadra, the Koyana, the Ghatprabha, the Musi, and the Bhima.
Its drainage basin is shared by Maharashtra, Karnataka, and Andhra Pradesh.
The Kaveri rises in the Brahmagri range of the Western Ghats and reaches the Bay of Bengal south of Cuddalore in Tamil Nadu. The total length is about 760 km.
Main tributaries are the Amravati, the Bhavani, the Hemavati, and the Kabini. Its basin drains parts of Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu.

content.title: Shivasamudram Falls
content.text: The Kaveri makes the second-largest waterfall in India — Shivasamudram Falls. Hydroelectric power generated from the falls is supplied to Mysuru, Bengaluru, and the Kolar Gold Field.
Besides the major rivers mentioned, several smaller rivers flow towards the east. Notable examples include the Damoder, the Brahmani, the Baitarni, and the Subarnarekha.

India has many lakes that differ from each other in size and other characteristics. Most lakes are permanent; some contain water only during the rainy season, particularly in semi-arid regions with inland drainage.

Lakes have been formed through various processes: glacial action (ice sheets and glaciers), wind action, river action, and human activities. Understanding these formation processes helps explain why different lakes have different characteristics and locations.

topics.topic_name: Types of Lakes Based on Formation

content.text: Glacial lakes are formed when glaciers dug out basins, which were later filled with snowmelt. Most freshwater lakes are in the Himalayan region and are of glacial origin. Examples include lakes in Kashmir and Himachal Pradesh.
Oxbow lakes form when a meandering river across a floodplain creates cut-offs that develop into curved lakes. These are common in plains areas where rivers have well-developed meanders.
Coastal lakes form when spits and bars (depositional landforms) form lagoons in coastal areas. Examples include Chilika lake, Pulicat lake, and Kolleru lake.
Lakes in regions of inland drainage are sometimes seasonal. The Sambhar lake in Rajasthan is an example of a salt water lake whose water is used for salt production.
Artificial lakes have been created by damming rivers for hydroelectric power generation. Examples include Guru Gobind Sagar (Bhakra Nangal Project).
The Wular lake in Jammu and Kashmir is the largest freshwater lake in India, formed as a result of tectonic activity (movement of earth's crust) rather than glacial or other processes.
Other important freshwater lakes include the Dal lake, Bhimtal, Nainital, Loktak, and Barapani. These lakes support tourism and have ecological significance.

Lakes are of great value to human beings. A lake helps regulate the flow of a river. During heavy rains, it prevents flooding by absorbing excess water. During dry season, it helps maintain an even flow of water in rivers.

Lakes can be used for developing hydel (hydroelectric) power. They moderate the climate of surrounding areas, maintain the aquatic ecosystem, enhance natural beauty, help develop tourism, and provide recreation facilities.

notes.section_title: Role of Rivers and Drainage in Economy and Society

content.text: Rivers have been of fundamental importance throughout human history. Water from rivers is a basic natural resource essential for various human activities. Therefore, riverbanks have attracted settlers from ancient times. These settlements have now become big cities.

Using rivers for irrigation, navigation, and hydropower generation is of special significance — particularly to a country like India, where agriculture is the major source of livelihood for millions of people.

The growing domestic, municipal, industrial, and agricultural demand for water from rivers affects water quality. More water is drained from rivers, reducing their volume. Heavy loads of untreated sewage and industrial effluents are emptied into rivers, affecting water quality and the river's self-cleansing capacity.

For example, given adequate streamflow, the Ganga water can dilute and assimilate pollution loads within 20 km of large cities. However, increasing urbanization and industrialization prevent this natural process, and pollution levels in many rivers have been rising.

content.title: National River Conservation Plan (NRCP)
content.text: The river cleaning programme was initiated with the launching of the Ganga Action Plan (GAP) in 1985. The GAP was expanded to cover other rivers under the National River Conservation Plan (NRCP) in 1995. The objective is to improve water quality of major river systems through pollution abatement work.

glossary.definition: The river system of an area; the network of streams and rivers that collectively drain water from a region into larger water bodies.
The area drained by a single river system, encompassing all land whose water flows into a particular river.
An elevated area such as a mountain or upland that separates two drainage basins; determines which river system water will flow into.
A river that has water flowing throughout the year, receiving water from both rainfall and melted snow from mountains.
A river whose flow is dependent on rainfall; water volume reduces significantly or disappears during dry seasons.
A deep, narrow valley with steep sides, typically formed when a river cuts through hard rock layers.
A snake-like bend or winding curve in a river's course, typically formed in the middle and lower courses of rivers on gentle slopes.
A crescent-shaped lake formed when a meander in a river is cut off from the main channel, leaving a curved body of water.
A low-lying area where a river deposits sediments as it enters a larger water body, typically fan-shaped with multiple channels (distributaries).
A river channel that flows away from the main river, typically found in deltas where rivers divide before reaching the ocean.
A wider mouth area where a river meets the sea, characterized by the mixing of fresh and salt water; formed by river erosion rather than sediment deposition.
A main river together with all its tributaries; an organized network of flowing water draining a region.
A stream or river that flows into a larger river, contributing water to the main channel.
A depression in the earth's crust formed by geological faulting, through which rivers like Narmada and Tapi flow.
A river channel divided into multiple threads or islands, characteristic of rivers with high sediment loads like the Brahmaputra.
An island formed in the middle of a river channel, created by deposits of sediment; the largest is Majuli in the Brahmaputra.
The source streams of a river, typically originating in mountains or highlands.
A large mass of ice that moves slowly down mountains; melting glaciers feed perennial rivers like the Ganga and Brahmaputra.
A lake created by human activity, such as by damming rivers for hydroelectric power generation.
A lake formed in a basin created by glacial action, typically found in mountainous regions and containing freshwater.
A lake formed in coastal areas when spits and bars create lagoons, mixing freshwater and saltwater.
A drainage system in arid and semi-arid regions where rivers do not reach the sea but instead drain into inland lakes or disappear through evaporation.
An elevated terrain that separates two drainage basins; determines the direction water flows and which river system it enters.
A tree-like drainage pattern where tributaries spread in different directions, resembling branches of a tree before meeting the main river.

summary_points: Drainage refers to a region's river system; a drainage basin is the area drained by a single river system; a water divide (mountain or upland) separates two drainage basins.

Indian rivers are classified into two major groups: Himalayan rivers (perennial, long courses, heavy erosion in upper reaches, well-developed deltas) and Peninsular rivers (mostly seasonal, shorter courses, less erosive power).

Three major Himalayan rivers are the Indus (2,900 km, one-third basin in India), Ganga (2,500 km, formed by Bhagirathi and Alaknanda), and Brahmaputra (longest but mostly outside India, forms Sundarban Delta).

The Ganga basin is the largest in India; major tributaries include Yamuna (right bank), Ghaghara, Gandak, Kosi from Nepal, and Chambal, Betwa, Son from peninsular uplands; forms world's largest and fastest-growing Sundarban Delta.

The Brahmaputra flows through Tibet (Tsang Po), Arunachal Pradesh (Dihang), and Assam; known for braided channels, riverine islands (largest: Majuli), and frequent flooding; carries significant silt from its course.

Major Peninsular rivers flow eastward into Bay of Bengal: Godavari (largest peninsular river, called Dakshin Ganga), Mahanadi, Krishna, Kaveri; Narmada and Tapi flow westward into Arabian Sea through rift valleys and estuaries.

Water divide in Peninsular India is formed by Western Ghats running north-south; most peninsular rivers originate here; Narmada and Tapi are only long westward-flowing rivers, forming estuaries not deltas.

Lakes in India are formed through different processes: glacial action (Himalayan freshwater lakes), river meandering (oxbow lakes), coastal deposition (Chilika, Pulicat, Kolleru), tectonic activity (Wular lake—largest freshwater), and human damming (Guru Gobind Sagar).
Lakes regulate river flow (preventing floods in monsoon, maintaining flow in dry season), generate hydroelectric power, moderate climate, maintain aquatic ecosystems, and support tourism.
Rivers have attracted human settlements since ancient times, becoming major cities; they are essential for irrigation, navigation, and hydropower — crucial for India's agricultural economy.

River pollution from untreated sewage and industrial effluents threatens water quality and rivers' self-cleansing capacity; Ganga Action Plan (1985) and National River Conservation Plan (1995) aim to improve river water quality.
